title: Astrophysics for People in a Hurry

chapters.title: Around fourteen billion years ago the universe was very different
chapters.text:
Understanding where the universe came from can help us find our place in the cosmos and answer basic questions about existence, meaning, and purpose. What do we know so far? Long ago, space, matter, and energy were packed into an unimaginably tiny volume. The mass was so small you could not see it. Look at the period at the end of this sentence. Now imagine a clump a trillionth of that size, and you have a sense of how small it was. Every part of the universe was scorching hot, and everything existed as a single, unified whole. There was only one way out, and that was a Big Bang.

As Lucretius once wrote, the world has persisted for many a long year, having once been set going in the appropriate motions, and from these everything else follows.

The expansion gave birth to the universe. A trillionth of a second later, the cosmos was made of photons, because subatomic particles and electromagnetic energy were in constant interaction. The heat of the universe turned photon energy into particles of matter and antimatter. These pairs then collided and annihilated each other, releasing their energy back into photons.

As the cosmos cooled and was no longer hot enough to destroy quarks, the photons transformed into a new family of particles called hadrons. This step produced protons and neutrons, the building blocks of the atom.

Protons and neutrons sit at the center of the atom, where most of its mass is concentrated.

What came before all this? No one knows. But we can say with confidence that the universe began and that it has been growing ever since.

chapters.title: The laws of physics hold everywhere
chapters.text:
Before Sir Isaac Newton proposed his universal law of gravity, no one thought that science applied everywhere. Newton showed that all motion is predictable and understandable. He realized that the entire Earth is governed by gravity, from apples falling off trees to the moon that stays close as a faithful companion.

One of the most powerful traits of physical laws is their universality. That quality drives scientific discovery at incredible speed. For example, the rules that shape the spectral signatures on the sun are the same ones that shape them on Earth. Even though they sit about ninety three million miles apart, they obey the same physics.

Even with the help of universality, many secrets of the universe remain unsolved. We do not yet have solid evidence of conscious life on other planets or in distant galaxies. Any alien life form might operate in ways we cannot yet imagine. Physically, though, any alien civilization should run on the same physical principles as our planet.

Perhaps the most intriguing mystery of all is dark matter. Is there a way to experience it? Unfortunately, not yet. We can only account for about fifteen percent of the gravity in the universe, the kind that acts on visible objects. Dark matter remains hidden from us.

The laws of physics do not need an enforcement agency to keep them current. The basic constants we know are independent of time and place. They are always present and always working. That is why universal physical laws and constants are so important to science. One example is the gravitational constant, often called big G, which measures the strength of gravitational force.

The gravitational constant, a sign of the universe homogeneity, helps determine how brightly a star shines.

Light is another essential topic in any discussion of the universe. Everyone knows how fast it travels. No matter how hard you run, you will never beat light in a race. Nothing in the universe has been observed exceeding the speed of light.

The speed of gravity matches the speed of light, making gravity the only known phenomenon that can match light pace.

chapters.title: The discovery of the cosmic microwave background
chapters.text:
Let us go back to the beginning. After the Big Bang, the universe started to expand. All the energy packed into a tight space began its long journey across the cosmos. As a result, the universe shrank in density, grew colder, and darkened, and it stayed that way for about three hundred eighty thousand years.

Atoms formed when the temperature dropped low enough. Particles slowed down as things cooled, and electrons lost their frantic pace. Protons were finally able to catch up with them. This happened when the universe temperature fell to about three thousand Kelvin. That moment marked the birth of atoms, the smallest piece of a chemical element that can exist on its own.

Because photons were finally free, the cosmos expanded by a factor of one thousand. The cosmic background cooled in proportion. The energy of every visible light photon dropped to a thousandth of what it had been. These photons earned a new name: the cosmic microwave background, or CMB. With a standard telescope, the background between stars and galaxies looks completely black. A sensitive radio telescope, however, can pick up a faint, uniform glow. That light does not belong to any star or galaxy. It is what the CMB looks like.

In the 1940s, the American physicist George Gamow and his colleagues predicted the existence of the CMB. Two other American physicists, Ralph Alpher and Robert Herman, were the first to estimate the temperature of the cosmic background radiation.

The CMB is the cooled remnant of the first light to shine in the universe. It is evidence of the Big Bang origin of the cosmos, an echo of the great explosion. The early universe was denser, hotter, and filled with an opaque cloud of hydrogen plasma. As the universe expanded, the plasma cooled, and the radiation inside it stretched into longer wavelengths. Eventually, protons and electrons joined to form neutral hydrogen atoms.

Every human contains matter. We are physical beings made of atoms, the building blocks of matter.

The first direct detection of the cosmic microwave background happened by accident in 1964, when scientists at Bell Telephone Laboratories, the research arm of AT&T, picked up the signal.

chapters.title: The universe contains more than meets the eye
chapters.text:
In cosmology, galaxies get most of the attention simply because they are easy to see. Yet between the galaxies, the cosmos may contain all sorts of hard to detect objects.

The Milky Way earned its name because it looks like a stream of spilled milk stretched across the night sky.

Many people assume that space is empty, but research tells a different story. Current theories and instruments let us explore the universe and make remarkable discoveries. Among them are runaway stars, dwarf galaxies, dark matter, and faint blue galaxies. The evidence suggests the real action is happening between galaxies, not inside them.

There are many more dwarf galaxies than their larger counterparts.

The Milky Way contains about two hundred to four hundred billion stars. Divide that number by two hundred thousand, and you get a sense of how few stars a typical dwarf galaxy holds. Smaller galaxies behave a bit like stars in their location, swirling around bigger ones. The famous Magellanic Clouds are part of the Milky Way dwarf galaxy family. You can see them from Earth with the naked eye, but only if you live in the Southern Hemisphere. Their story goes far back. Indigenous peoples in South America and Africa have observed the Magellanic Clouds since ancient times.

As Neil deGrasse Tyson has put it, we do not simply live in the universe. The universe lives within us.

Dwarf galaxies sometimes have a finite life. When two or more galaxies collide, hundreds of millions of stars can form, often with destructive effects such as violent gas cloud collisions. Those collisions send a flood of cosmic particles drifting into intergalactic space, and no one knows how many are out there. Measuring a single star is hard, since they are not bright enough to image clearly. Astronomers can only work from the faint glow the stars give off.

Cosmic rays are extremely high energy, fast moving, charged subatomic particles that stream through space on a regular basis.

Vacuum energy, an unusual presence between galaxies, behaves like a seething ocean of virtual particles. It acts as a kind of counter gravitational pressure that thrives where matter is sparse. This effect may be the fuel that drives dark energy.

chapters.title: Dark matter is not really dark
chapters.text:
How does ordinary matter differ from the dark kind? It depends on the astrophysical environment, but the difference is most striking in galaxies and clusters of stars.

Dark matter is a hypothetical form that makes up roughly eighty five percent of all matter in the universe.

What is interesting about dark matter is that it does not seem to interact with gravity in the way visible matter does, and it does not affect how the moon circles the Earth or how the planets travel around the sun. Since dark matter does not influence Earth gravity, does that mean gravity works differently on cosmic scales than on Earth? Not necessarily. Scientists have not yet figured out what dark matter is made of. We can be sure, though, that life, planets, and the stuff of stars are just a small slice of the cosmic cake.

Matter in the cosmos condensed into clumps that formed clusters and superclusters of galaxies about five hundred thousand years after the Big Bang. The size of the universe doubled and kept growing. In the past, people believed the universe was stable and unchanging. We now know that belief is wrong.

Every body in the solar system, not counting the sun, adds up to less than two tenths of a percent of the sun mass.

Two opposing forces are at work in the universe. Gravity tries to pull everything together, and expansion tries to dilute it. Dark matter is what allows gravity to win that battle. Without it, we would not exist.

How much dark matter does gravity need? About six times the amount of energy supplied by ordinary matter. That is how we know dark matter exists. Even without knowing exactly what it is, we can be confident that it is not our enemy. Our calculations need to match what we see in the cosmos, and pinning down the nature of dark matter will take time and resources. It is a journey worth taking.

What else separates ordinary matter from dark matter? If most dark matter took part in nuclear fusion, helium would outnumber hydrogen, since fusion favors the production of helium. Because we do not see that pattern, we can assume that dark matter does not take part in nuclear fusion and cannot be called ordinary matter.

chapters.title: The ongoing search for answers and what we have learned
chapters.text:
Our modern understanding of gravity is rooted in the general theory of relativity, written down by Albert Einstein in 1916. That theory explains the mathematics of how matter behaves under the influence of gravity. In the 1920s, the theory of quantum mechanics was developed, clarifying the behavior of molecules and atoms. The two frameworks are philosophically incompatible. Scientists have tried to combine them into a single theory of quantum gravity, but those efforts have not yet succeeded.

In 2016, gravitational waves were detected for the first time using a specially designed observatory. As Einstein had predicted, the waves are ripples in the fabric of space time, traveling at the speed of light. They are produced by violent gravitational events, such as the collision of two black holes.

Another foundational discovery concerns the solar system. Nicolaus Copernicus showed that it is heliocentric, with the planets orbiting the sun. This explained why planets move in their observed paths, though it caused some confusion in the scientific world. The general idea of orbiting the sun was correct, but the shape of the orbits was off. Planets do not travel in perfect circles. They travel in ellipses, which are flattened circles. His core idea was right. It just needed a few adjustments.

Einstein conception of gravity transformed how we see the universe. He moved away from Newton idea of gravity as a mysterious force acting at a distance. In Einstein view, gravity is the way matter responds to curves in space and time. Mass creates dimples in the fabric of the universe, in space and time themselves. Matter and space have a two way relationship. Space tells matter how to move, and mass curves space and time in return.

Gravity also affects time, although the effect happens on such a large scale that we rarely notice it.

In 1929, the American astronomer Edwin P. Hubble established that the cosmos is not static. He measured the position of galaxies in relation to the Milky Way and found that the farther away a galaxy is, the faster it moves away from us. The universe is not fixed. It is growing.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The universe is a mysterious creature, and centuries of careful research have not come close to answering every question. Yet there is beauty in the puzzle. So much possibility for exploration lies within the reach of the human race.

Astrophysics uses the principles of physics to analyze and understand the phenomena and objects of astronomy. It aims to explain how astronomical objects and events occur and how they work.

To pursue this work, we need to place living above surviving. Countries must value the exploration of outer space. A culture that honors the thrill and the cost of discovery pushes the limits of knowledge and breaks through them. Curiosity is one of the most beautiful traits of humanity, and if we encourage it, we will get excellent results.

The study of astrophysics is essential to understanding human existence. Despite religious assumptions, cosmic knowledge is what reveals the truth about the universe. There is much to learn, and much to unlearn, about the galaxies. Influenced by religious traditions with their own creation stories, the Big Bang Theory remains one of the most controversial explanations of where the universe came from. Many of our actions are shaped by the false notion that the world revolves around us, a belief reinforced by religious and cultural teachings. There is plenty of evidence about how the world came to be. We will keep overlooking that evidence until we make a conscious effort to learn and to teach others the scientific story of creation.

A few practical steps can help. Spend time thinking about the cosmic truths around you. Learn more about astrophysics to understand the fundamental reality of human existence. To dig deeper into space and astrophysics, visit StarTalk, Neil deGrasse Tyson educational YouTube channel.

Keep reading to the end.
